stances.claim: He initially participated in student activism because, as an impoverished youth, he was enamored by Karl Marx's 'from each according to his ability, to each according to his needs' as a solution to poverty and economic inequality.

Two people dissuaded him from Marxism: Fr. Michael Nolan (FEU chaplain) who taught catechesis and proved communism 'theoretically attractive but not implementable' by pointing to Red China and Russia; and Elenita Carpio (his future wife) who 'tenderly opened the clenched fist of this activist' over three years of courtship.
stances.rhetorical_move: personal-conversion narrative with two named agents

stances.claim: Student activism in the Philippines owes its origin to Wenceslao Vinzons (1941 anti-Japanese resistance), bloomed in peacetime during the late 1950s under his own leadership, and reached fruition in the First Quarter Storm of January 26 - March 17, 1970 against Marcos Sr.

Today's Philippine student leaders are 'not (maybe not yet)' heavily involved in Israel-Hamas, Russia-Ukraine, or WPS — but he expects them to resume activism when they realize there is 'no dispute so remote and so misunderstood they would not take a stand on.'

register_markers: STUDENT ACTIVISM HERE OWES ITS ORIGIN
Tita (familial vocative for Henrietta de Villa)
decision_framework_signals: applies personal-conversion narrative (Marx → Christ) via two named persuaders as transmission-of-values framework
frames Filipino student activism's origins genealogically (Vinzons → his own 1950s peacetime activism → First Quarter Storm)
uses ideological-testability test (theoretical appeal vs implementation) to refute Marxism
uses 'no dispute so remote' framing to predict activism's perpetual resurrection
notable_anecdotes: Hawking newspapers and selling textbooks to law-school classmates while enamored by the Marxist slogan 'From each according to his ability, to each according to his needs'
Fr. Michael Nolan as FEU chaplain teaching him catechesis and refuting Marxism by pointing him to study the economic reality in Red China and Russia
Meeting his future wife Leni — editor of the 'Scholastican' and aspiring Benedictine nun — as his 'accidental seatmate' on a 10-hour Manila-to-Naga train ride for a NUSP seminar
Leni planning to convert 'this amateur admirer of Karl Marx into an obedient disciple of our Lord Jesus Christ'
Winning Leni's heart over three years 'to the chagrin of the St. Scho nuns who expected her to join them in blessed singlehood'
Leni 'opening the clenched fist of this activist' so he resisted temptations to return to sociopolitical arena, devoting himself instead to judiciary, private sector, family, and the Lord
Henrietta de Villa occupying the Pontifical Council for the Laity slot until she joined the diplomatic service in 1996
